items.creator: Bob Dylan
items.title: Subterranean Homesick Blues
items.quote: Don’t need a weatherman to know … way the wind blows
items.reference: Bob Dylan, “Subterranean Homesick Blues,” on Bringing It All Back Home, Columbia Records, 1965. Written by Bob Dylan.
items.comment: If so, and if foresight people think they are weathermen, oh my gosh. Can one argue with that really? The useful question is what foresight adds when the wind is already felt: Do we need to accept it and reveal those who still listen to weathermen; ask them to look in the mirror?

items.creator: Bruce Springsteen
items.title: The River
items.quote: Is a dream a lie if it don't come true
items.reference: Bruce Springsteen, “The River,” on The River, Columbia Records, 1980. Written by Bruce Springsteen.
items.comment: A sharp line for futures work because it separates hope from fulfilment without making hope simple or innocent. It asks what happens to people, institutions, and promises when imagined futures do not arrive: were they false, or did they become something heavier?
questions: When does an unrealised future become a betrayal?
What do futures practices owe to disappointed dreams?
items.emphasis: What happens to a future when it fails to arrive?

note: Song and film excerpts are kept short; the work of the box is commentary and reference, not reproduction.
